1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	% HISTORICAL (abandoned route): an earlier draft built the glued sheaf as a hand-rolled
191	% presheaf of compatible families (decls gluePresheaf / gluePresheafModule / gluePresheafIsSheaf).
192	% That route is abandoned; the gluing is now the equalizer of pushforwards described in the
193	% Construction paragraph of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, which subsumes it.
194	
195	\begin{definition}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
199	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
201	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
202	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
203	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
204	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
205	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
206	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
207	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
208	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
209	  charts. Suppose given:
210	  \begin{itemize}
211	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
212	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
213	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
214	      \[
215	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
216	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
217	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
218	      \]
219	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
220	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
221	  \end{itemize}
222	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
223	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
224	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
225	  \((g_{ij})\):
226	  \begin{enumerate}
227	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
228	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
229	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
230	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
231	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
232	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
233	      isomorphisms satisfy
234	      \[
235	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
236	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
237	      \]
238	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
239	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
240	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
241	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
242	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
243	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
244	      condition.
245	  \end{enumerate}
246	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
247	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
248	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
249	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
250	
251	  \emph{Construction.} The category \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
252	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has all limits, and both pushforward along a morphism and
253	  these limits preserve the sheaf condition; the glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is therefore
254	  built directly as a limit -- an \emph{equalizer of pushforwards} -- rather than through a
255	  hand-built presheaf of compatible families. Concretely, form the two parallel maps of
256	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
257	  \[
258	    \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
259	      \;\;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\;\;
260	    \prod_{i,j} (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr),
261	  \]
262	  where, on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) pushes the \(i\)-th factor forward along the
263	  overlap immersion \(f_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction
264	  together with the pushforward-composition comparison
265	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\circ f_{ij}^{*}\) (the dual of
266	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), and leg \(b\) pushes the \(j\)-th factor forward and
267	  additionally transports it across the inverse transition isomorphism \((g_{ij})^{-1}\)
268	  and the glue datum's compatibility identity, so that both legs land on the common overlap
269	  sheaf \((\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i)\). The glued sheaf is the
270	  equalizer
271	  \[
272	    \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a,b) \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
273	  \]
274	  the sub-sheaf of tuples of pushed-forward chart sections whose two overlap restrictions
275	  agree -- precisely the compatible families, now realised as a categorical limit rather than
276	  a bespoke presheaf. The cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on the family \((g_{ij})\) are
277	  \emph{not} consumed in forming this equalizer object: the limit exists for any family of
278	  transition maps. They are instead what make the equalizer descend correctly, and are
279	  exactly the hypotheses pinned down downstream when the restriction isomorphisms
280	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso} are produced. This specialises effective descent
281	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
282	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
283	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
284	
285	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
286	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
287	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
288	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
289	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
290	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
291	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
292	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
293	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
294	\end{definition}
295	
296	\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
297	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}
299	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
300	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
301	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
302	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
303	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
305	  \[
306	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
307	  \]
308	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
309	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
310	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
311	\end{definition}

313	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
314	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
315	  By the equalizer-of-pushforwards description \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, a section of
316	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
317	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
318	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\) -- the \(i\)-th factor of the product
319	  \(\prod_i (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) into which the equalizer embeds; the
320	  chart-restriction bridge \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback
321	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection
322	  \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart
323	  section by the overlap relation).
324	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
325	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
326	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
327	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
328	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
329	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.

332	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
333	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
335	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
336	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
337	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
338	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
339	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
340	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
341	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
342	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
343	\end{lemma}
344	\begin{proof}
345	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
346	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
347	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
348	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
349	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
350	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
351	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
352	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
353	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
354	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
355	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
356	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
357	\end{proof}

359	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
360	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
362	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
364	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
365	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
366	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
367	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
368	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
369	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
370	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
371	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
372	  \[
373	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
374	    \qquad \text{for every } i.
375	  \]
376	\end{definition}

378	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
379	  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
380	  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
381	  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
382	  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
383	  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes

386	  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting

391	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
392	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
393	
394	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
395	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
396	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
397	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
398	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
399	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
400	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
401	
402	\begin{lemma}\leanok
403	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
404	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
406	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
407	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
408	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
413	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
414	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
415	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
416	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}\leanok
420	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
421	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
423	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
424	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
425	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
426	  \[
427	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
428	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
429	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
430	  \]
431	\end{lemma}
432	
433	\begin{proof}
434	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
435	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
436	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
437	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
438	\end{proof}
439	
440	\begin{lemma}\leanok
441	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
442	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
444	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
445	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
446	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
447	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
448	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
449	\end{lemma}
450	
451	\begin{proof}
452	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
453	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
454	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
455	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
456	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
457	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
458	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
459	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
460	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
461	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
462	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
463	\end{proof}

465	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
466	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
467	
468	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
469	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
470	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
471	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
472	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
473	
474	\begin{definition}\leanok
475	[Global section of the unit module]
476	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
478	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
479	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
480	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
481	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
482	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
483	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
484	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
485	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
486	  global function.
487	\end{definition}
488	
489	\begin{definition}\leanok
490	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
491	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
493	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
494	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
495	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
496	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
497	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
498	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
499	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
500	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
501	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
502	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
503	\end{definition}
504	
505	\begin{lemma}\leanok
506	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
507	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
509	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
510	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
511	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
512	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
513	\end{lemma}
514	
515	\begin{proof}
516	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
517	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
518	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
519	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{lemma}\leanok
523	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
524	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
526	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
527	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
528	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
529	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
534	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
535	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}[Composition of scalar endomorphisms is multiplication]
539	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_comp}
541	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
542	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the scalar endomorphisms compose by
543	  multiplication: \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) \circ \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) =
544	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a\,b)\).
545	\end{lemma}
546	\begin{proof}
547	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
548	  Under the identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the
549	  scalar endomorphisms act as multiplication by \(a\) and by \(b\); their composite
550	  is multiplication by \(a\,b\), because the section ring is commutative and
551	  restriction maps are ring homomorphisms.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\begin{lemma}[Scalar endomorphism is additive]
555	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_add}
557	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
558	  For \(a, b \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) one has \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a + b) =
559	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) + \mathrm{scalarEnd}(b)\).
560	\end{lemma}
561	\begin{proof}
562	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
563	  Multiplication by \(a + b\) equals multiplication by \(a\) plus multiplication by
564	  \(b\) on every section, since the restriction maps of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) are additive;
565	  under \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) this is the stated additivity.
566	\end{proof}
567	
568	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
569	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
570	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
571	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
572	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
573	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
574	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
575	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
576	
577	\begin{definition}[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
578	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
579	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
580	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
581	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
582	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
583	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
584	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
585	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
586	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
587	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
588	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
589	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
590	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
591	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
592	\end{definition}
593	
594	\begin{lemma}[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
595	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
596	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
597	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
598	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
599	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
600	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
601	  \[
602	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
603	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
604	  \]
605	\end{lemma}
606	\begin{proof}
607	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
608	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
609	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
610	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
611	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
612	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
613	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
614	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
615	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
616	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
617	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
618	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
619	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
620	\end{proof}
621	
622	\begin{lemma}[Matrix endomorphism of the identity is the identity]
623	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_one}
625	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
626	  The matrix endomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism of
627	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\): \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1_{d\times d}) =
628	  \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
629	\end{lemma}
630	\begin{proof}
631	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
632	  The diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\)
633	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the off-diagonal components are
634	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the morphism with this
635	  component matrix is the identity of the biproduct.
636	\end{proof}
637	
638	\begin{definition}[Free-sheaf automorphism of an invertible matrix]
639	  \label{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso}
641	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}
642	  Let \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) be mutually
643	  inverse, \(M N = 1\) and \(N M = 1\). The \emph{matrix automorphism}
644	  \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is the isomorphism of free sheaves
645	  \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) with forward map
646	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) and inverse map \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)\); the two
647	  composites are identities by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp} (turning the products
648	  \(NM\) and \(MN\) into the relevant compositions) and \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
649	  Its forward map is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) by construction
650	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}).
651	\end{definition}
652	
653	\begin{lemma}[Forward map of the matrix automorphism]
654	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_hom}
656	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
657	  The forward map of \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(M, N)\) is \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
658	\end{lemma}
659	\begin{proof}
660	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, def:gr_matrixEnd}
661	  Immediate from the definition \cref{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso}, whose forward field is
662	  literally \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\).
663	\end{proof}
664	
665	\begin{lemma}[Matrix automorphisms compose multiplicatively (L2: linkage)]
666	  \label{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul}
667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixToFreeIso_mul}
668	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
669	  Let \(A, A', B, B'\) be \(d \times d\) matrices of global functions on \(S\) with
670	  \(A A' = A' A = 1\), \(B B' = B' B = 1\), so that \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')\)
671	  and \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')\) are automorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\).
672	  Their forward maps compose to the forward map of the product automorphism, with the
673	  order reversed by the component contravariance:
674	  \[
675	    \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(A, A')_{\mathrm{hom}}
676	      \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}(B, B')_{\mathrm{hom}}
677	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B \cdot A).
678	  \]
679	  Equivalently, composition of matrix automorphisms realises matrix multiplication of
680	  the underlying matrices. This is the linkage that turns the matrix-level cocycle
681	  identity into a composition identity of sheaf-of-module isomorphisms.
682	\end{lemma}
683	\begin{proof}
684	  \uses{def:gr_matrixToFreeIso, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom, lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
685	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_hom} the two forward maps are
686	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\) and \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B)\); their composite is
687	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(A)\circ\mathrm{matrixEnd}(B) = \mathrm{matrixEnd}(B\cdot A)\) by
688	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}.
689	\end{proof}

695	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}

699	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from

701	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
702	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
703	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
704	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
705	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
706	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
707	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
708	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
709	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
710	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
711	  \[
712	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
713	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
714	  \]
715	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
716	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
717	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
718	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
719	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
720	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
721	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
722	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).

724	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
725	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
726	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
727	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
728	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
729	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
730	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
731	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
732	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
733	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
734	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
735	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
736	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
737	  free sheaves'' primitive.

775	\begin{lemma}\leanok
776	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
777	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
779	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
780	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
781	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
782	\end{lemma}
783	
784	\begin{proof}
785	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
786	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
787	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
788	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
789	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
790	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
791	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
792	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
793	  \[
794	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
795	  \]
796	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
797	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
798	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
799	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
800	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
801	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
802	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
803	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
804	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
805	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
806	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
807	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
808	\end{proof}

810	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
811	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
812	
813	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
814	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
815	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
816	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
817	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
818	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
819	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
820	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
821	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
822	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
823	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
824	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.
825	
826	\begin{definition}[The bundle transition \(g_{I,J}\)]
827	  \label{def:gr_bundleTransition}
828	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition}
829	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_scalarEnd, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
830	    lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition}
831	  Let \(I, J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) be size-\(d\) subsets and let
832	  \(f_{IJ} : U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) and \(t_{IJ} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\) be
833	  the overlap immersion and transition of the chart cover (\cref{def:gr_transition}). The
834	  \emph{bundle transition} \(g_{I,J}\) is the isomorphism of sheaves of modules on the
835	  overlap \(U^I_J\)
836	  \[
837	    g_{I,J} \;:\;
838	      f_{IJ}^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\bigr)
839	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
840	      (t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI})^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_{U^J}^{\,d}\bigr)
841	  \]
842	  induced by the invertible \(d \times d\) matrix
843	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J)\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
844	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor, which is invertible because
845	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}).
846	
847	  \emph{Realisation.} The transition is built exactly as the chart quotient
848	  \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}: each entry \((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q} \in R^I_J\) is a global
849	  function on the overlap, its scalar endomorphism
850	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}_{p,q}\bigr)\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the
851	  \((p,q)\)-component, and the universal property of the rank-\(d\) biproduct assembles
852	  these into an automorphism of the free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\); the
853	  source and target pullbacks of free sheaves are identified with this
854	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_J}^{\,d}\) through the free-pullback comparisons
855	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(f_{IJ}\) and \(t_{IJ} \circ f_{JI}\), conjugating
856	  the matrix automorphism into the displayed isomorphism. Invertibility of the matrix makes
857	  \(g_{I,J}\) an isomorphism.
858	\end{definition}
859	
860	\begin{lemma}[Self-identity of the bundle transition (C1)]
861	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}
862	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_self}
863	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self,
864	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
865	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
866	  The self-transition is the identity: \(g_{I,I} = \mathrm{id}\) on
867	  \(f_{II}^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\). This is the module-level form of the descent
868	  condition (C1) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\).
869	\end{lemma}

872	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
873	  The \(I\)-th minor of the universal matrix is the identity block, \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
874	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}), so its Cramer inverse is again the
875	  identity, \((X^I_I)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}).
876	  Hence in the biproduct presentation of \cref{def:gr_bundleTransition} the diagonal
877	  components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
878	  off-diagonal components are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}), so
879	  the assembled automorphism is the identity of \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I_I}^{\,d}\); conjugation by

883	\begin{lemma}[Cramer-inverse cocycle on the triple overlap (L1: matrix identity)]
884	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}
885	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_matrix}
886	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
887	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
888	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
889	  Over the common triple-overlap ring (the localisation carrying the three charts'
890	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
891	  multiplicative cocycle identity
892	  \[
893	    (X^J_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}.
894	  \]
895	  This is the pure matrix-algebra core of (C2), independent of any sheaf data.
896	\end{lemma}
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
899	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
900	    lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_cocycle}
901	  Write all matrices over the common triple-overlap ring. The image matrix of the
902	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is
903	  computed by restricting to the \(K\)-indexed columns; since taking a column
904	  submatrix commutes with left multiplication (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}, the
905	  matrix form of \(\mathrm{Matrix.submatrix\_mul}\)),
906	  \[
907	    X^J_K \;=\; \bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_K .
908	  \]
909	  Both \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\) have unit determinant
910	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so this product of invertibles inverts
911	  by \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\):
912	  \[
913	    (X^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1}\,(X^I_J).
914	  \]
915	  Multiplying on the right by \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) and cancelling \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1\)
916	  via the two-sided Cramer identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}, the
917	  cancellation packaged in \cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}) gives
918	  \((X^J_K)^{-1} (X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\). The agreement of the three image
919	  matrices over the triple overlap that licenses these manipulations is the content of
920	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}, the matrix form of the scheme-level cocycle
921	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}.
922	\end{proof}

1028	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1029	  \[
1030	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1031	  \]
1032	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1033	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1034	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1035	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1036	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1037	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
1038	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
1039	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
1040	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
1041	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
1042	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
1043	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
1044	\end{definition}

1046	\begin{definition}

1048	\leanok
1049	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
1050	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
1051	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
1052	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
1053	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
1054	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
1055	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
1056	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1057	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1058	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
1059	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1060	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
1061	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
1062	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1063	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1064	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1065	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1066	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1067	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1068	  \[
1069	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1070	  \]
1071	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1072	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
1073	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1074	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1075	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1076	  \[
1077	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1078	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},

1081	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1082	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1083	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1084	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1085	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.

1088	\section{The functor of points}
1089	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1090	
1091	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1092	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1093	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1094	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1095	identities that make that action functorial.
1096	
1097	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1098	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1099	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1100	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1101	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1102	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1103	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1104	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1105	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1106	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1107	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1108	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1109	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1110	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1111	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1112	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1113	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1114	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1115	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1116	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1117	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1118	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1119	  functor's value at \(T\).
1120	
1121	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1122	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1123	  \[
1124	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1125	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1126	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1127	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1128	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1129	  \]
1130	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1131	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1132	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1133	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1134	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1135	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1136	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1137	  map of equivalence classes.
1138	\end{definition}
1139	
1140	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1141	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1142	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
1143	
1144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1145	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1146	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1148	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1149	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1150	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1151	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1152	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1153	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1154	\end{lemma}
1155	
1156	\begin{proof}
1157	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1158	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1159	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1160	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1161	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1162	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1163	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1164	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1165	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1166	\end{proof}
1167	
1168	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1169	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1170	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1172	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1173	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1174	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1175	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1176	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1177	\end{lemma}
1178	
1179	\begin{proof}
1180	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1181	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1182	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1183	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1184	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1185	\end{proof}
1186	
1187	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1188	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1189	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1191	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}

1193	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1194	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1195	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1196	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).

1198	
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1201	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1202	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1203	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1204	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1205	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1206	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1207	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1208	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1209	  well-defined.

1212	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1213	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1214	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}

1231	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1232	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1233	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1235	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}

1258	\begin{definition}
1259	
1260	\leanok
1261	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1262	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1264	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1265	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1266	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1267	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1268	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1269	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1270	  % "represents the
1271	  %  contravariant functor
1272	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1273	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1274	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1275	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1276	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1277	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1278	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1279	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1280	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1281	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1282	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1283	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1284	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1285	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1286	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1287	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1288	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1289	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1290	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1291	  \[
1292	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1293	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1294	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1295	  \]
1296	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1297	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1298	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1299	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1300	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1301	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1302	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1303	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1304	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1305	\end{definition}

1307	\section{The universal property}
1308	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1309	
1310	\begin{theorem}
1311	
1312	\leanok
1313	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1314	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1315	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1316	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1317	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1318	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1319	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1320	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1321	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1322	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1323	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1324	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1325	  %  contravariant functor
1326	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1327	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1328	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1329	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1330	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1331	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1332	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1333	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1334	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1335	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1336	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1337	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1338	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1339	  \[
1340	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1341	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1342	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1343	    \qquad
1344	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1345	      \;\longmapsto\;
1346	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1347	  \]
1348	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1349	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1350	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1351	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1352	  universal family.
1353	\end{theorem}
1354	
1355	\begin{proof}
1356	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1357	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1358	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1359	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1360	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1361	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1362	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1363	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1364	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1365	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1366	
1367	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1368	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1369	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1370	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1371	  the composite
1372	  \[
1373	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1374	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1375	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1376	  \]
1377	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1378	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1379	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1380	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1381	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1382	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1383	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1384	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
